skill.name: technical-blog-writing
skill.description: Guides technical blog writing for developers, including article structure, runnable examples, diagrams, benchmarks, and distribution workflows.
skill.summary: Write clearer developer blog posts with structured guidance for tutorials, deep dives, architecture posts, and benchmarks.
skill.author: inference-sh-9

security_audit.summary: AI review confirms some static findings are real publish risks: the skill includes a remote curl-to-shell install command and encourages authenticated external CLI workflows. Most Ruby backtick, weak cryptography, and reconnaissance detections are false positives caused by Markdown fences, SHA-256 verification text, examples, and prose. No prompt injection or confirmed malicious intent was found.

high_findings.title: Remote Install Script Piped to Shell
high_findings.description: The Quick Start tells users to run curl against https://cli.inference.sh and pipe the response directly into sh. This is dangerous because a compromised server, network path, or script change could execute arbitrary code before review. The later SHA-256 note reduces risk only for manual installation, not for the pipe-to-shell command.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The exact command uses curl piped into sh, and the surrounding text confirms it installs an external CLI. This is a known high-risk execution pattern even without malicious intent.

medium_findings.title: External CLI Actions Can Reach Third-Party Services
medium_findings.description: The skill allows Bash commands matching infsh and provides examples that search the web, generate images, execute Python through an app, and post to X. These workflows may transmit prompts, article drafts, generated content, or account actions to external services, so users need clear consent and credential boundaries.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The allowed-tools declaration permits infsh commands, and the examples clearly invoke remote apps and a posting workflow. The risk is strong but appears tied to intended functionality, not covert exfiltration.

low_findings.title: Markdown Fences Misclassified as Ruby Backticks
low_findings.description: Many static external command findings point to Markdown code fences, structure templates, prose examples, or inline code. These are documentation syntax and writing guidance, not Ruby backtick execution.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged locations are Markdown documentation blocks, not executable Ruby source. Some bash examples remain risky separately, but the Ruby backtick interpretation is not supported.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Detections Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The only security-relevant cryptographic reference is SHA-256 checksum verification for manual install. Other flagged lines are headings, article structures, or writing advice and do not implement cryptography.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed context contains no MD5, SHA-1, or cryptographic implementation. SHA-256 is used as a verification claim, which is not weak cryptography.

low_findings.title: System Reconnaissance Detections Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The scanner flagged architecture diagram HTML that names components such as Client, Edge, API, and Database. This is illustrative blog content, not host reconnaissance or environment collection.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line 242 is a static HTML diagram example embedded in a command input. There is no command that gathers system information from the local machine.

dangerous_patterns.title: Pipe to Shell Installation Pattern
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill includes a curl command piped into sh for installing an external CLI. This pattern executes remote content immediately and should be replaced with a verified download workflow.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The dangerous pattern is present verbatim on the flagged line. The risk is intrinsic to the execution pattern.

dangerous_patterns.title: Authenticated External Posting Command
dangerous_patterns.description: The distribution section shows an infsh command that creates a post on X. This can perform external account actions and should require explicit user approval before execution.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command name and input text indicate a social post creation action. It is likely intended behavior, but it affects an external account.

content.value_statement: Developer articles often fail when they lack structure, runnable examples, or clear trade-offs. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code practical guidance for planning, drafting, and improving technical blog posts.
actual_capabilities: Outlines tutorials, deep dives, incident reports, benchmarks, and architecture posts.
Gives developer-focused voice, tone, structure, and explanation-depth rules.
Defines standards for runnable code examples, version pinning, and outputs.
Suggests diagram and visual formats for architecture, flows, and comparisons.
Provides distribution guidance for blogs, developer communities, and social channels.
limitations: It does not fact-check technical claims without external research or user-provided sources.
It does not test code examples unless the assistant runs separate verification steps.
Some examples depend on the inference.sh CLI and external services.
It does not replace editorial review for brand voice, legal claims, or publication policy.

use_cases.title: Plan an Engineering Tutorial
use_cases.description: Turn a working implementation into a clear tutorial with prerequisites, steps, code examples, and next actions.

use_cases.title: Draft an Architecture Deep Dive
use_cases.description: Explain a system design decision with context, diagrams, trade-offs, outcomes, and lessons learned.

use_cases.title: Improve Developer Marketing Content
use_cases.description: Make a technical article more useful by reducing filler, adding evidence, and clarifying the target audience.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Blog Outline
prompt_templates.prompt: Create an outline for a technical blog post about [topic]. The audience is [audience level]. Include the title, TL;DR, sections, code example ideas, and required diagrams.

prompt_templates.title: Draft a Tutorial
prompt_templates.prompt: Write a developer tutorial for [task]. Include prerequisites, setup, step-by-step implementation, expected outputs, common errors, and next steps.

prompt_templates.title: Revise for Developers
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this draft for a developer audience. Remove filler, flag unsupported claims, improve code explanation, add trade-offs, and suggest clearer headings.

prompt_templates.title: Build a Full Publication Plan
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a publication plan for a technical article about [topic]. Include positioning, source research needs, article structure, diagrams, code verification steps, SEO terms, and distribution channels.

output_examples.input: A request to write a post about reducing API latency with edge caching.
output_examples.output: A structured article plan with a direct title, TL;DR, architecture section, benchmark section, trade-offs, diagram ideas, and further reading.

output_examples.input: A rough tutorial draft that starts with generic background and has incomplete examples.
output_examples.output: An edited version that states the outcome first, lists prerequisites, adds expected results, and explains why each step matters.

output_examples.input: A benchmark article idea comparing web frameworks.
output_examples.output: A methodology-first outline with reproducible test conditions, results tables, caveats, and a recommendation section.

best_practices: State the audience level, assumptions, and expected outcome near the start.
Use runnable examples with version numbers, expected outputs, and error handling.
Include evidence, trade-offs, and limitations before making recommendations.
anti_patterns: Do not open with generic industry background before showing the technical value.
Do not present snippets without context, setup steps, or expected results.
Do not make performance or reliability claims without measurements or sources.

faq.question: What types of posts does this skill support?
faq.answer: It supports tutorials, explainers, incident reports, benchmarks, architecture posts, and developer-focused distribution plans.

faq.question: Can it write complete technical articles?
faq.answer: Yes. It can draft complete articles when you provide the topic, audience, technical details, and source material.

faq.question: Does it verify code examples?
faq.answer: No. It recommends runnable examples, but code must be tested separately by the assistant or the user.

faq.question: Does it require the inference.sh CLI?
faq.answer: The writing guidance does not require it, but several examples use inference.sh commands for research, images, and distribution.

faq.question: Is this skill safe to publish?
faq.answer: Not without changes. The audit found a high-risk curl-to-shell install command and external CLI actions.

faq.question: What should be changed before publication?
faq.answer: Replace the pipe-to-shell install command with verified manual installation steps and require approval for external posting commands.
